title: The Future of AI in Everyday Life
description: We sit down with Sam Altman to explore how artificial intelligence is quietly reshaping everything from how we work to how we connect with each other — and what that means for the next decade.
summary: A wide-ranging conversation covering AGI timelines, the societal impact of automation, and why Sam believes curiosity is the most important human trait in an AI-dominated world.
transcript: Welcome to PodcastHub. Today I'm sitting down with Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI. Sam, thanks for being here. Great to be here. Let's start with the big question everyone's asking — when do you think we actually hit AGI? I think the framing of AGI as a single moment is a bit misleading...

title: Building Products People Actually Love
description: Julie Zhuo shares the framework she developed over a decade at Meta for building products that create genuine emotional connection — not just utility.
summary: Julie breaks down why most products fail at the empathy layer, how to run design reviews that actually improve outcomes, and her contrarian take on user research.

timestamps.chapter: What makes people love a product?

transcript: Julie, you spent over a decade at Meta. What's the single most important lesson you learned about product? Without question — the product that wins is the one that makes people feel something...

title: Startups, Failure, and What Comes After
description: Sahil Lavingia talks candidly about laying off 75% of Gumroad's team, the years of public failure that followed, and how he rebuilt — both the company and himself.
summary: One of the most honest conversations about entrepreneurship you'll hear. Sahil doesn't sugarcoat anything — from the day he had to fire his team to the identity crisis that followed.
transcript: Sahil, let's start at the lowest point. Walk me through the day you had to lay off your team. It was a Tuesday in January. I remember the exact temperature outside...

title: The Science of Deep Work
description: Cal Newport makes the case that the ability to focus without distraction is becoming the superpower of the modern economy — and explains exactly how to cultivate it.
summary: Cal walks through the neuroscience of focus, why open offices are destroying cognitive performance, and the four rituals he uses to protect his own deep work schedule.
transcript: Cal, you've been writing about focus for over a decade. Has the problem gotten worse? Dramatically. When I wrote Deep Work in 2016 smartphones were bad. Now they're inescapable...

title: Climate Tech: The Decade That Decides Everything
description: Nan Ransohoff explains why the 2020s are the most important decade in human history for climate — and what the tech industry's role looks like from inside Stripe's climate program.
summary: A clear-eyed, optimistic look at carbon removal, the economics of climate tech, and why Nan believes we have everything we need to solve this — except urgency.
transcript: Nan, you say the 2020s decide everything. That's a bold claim. Is it hyperbole? Not even slightly. The IPCC has been clear — the carbon budget closes by 2030 unless we act...

title: Writing That Actually Changes Minds
description: Anne Lamott talks about the messy, uncomfortable, deeply human process of writing — and why the first draft will always be terrible, and that's perfectly fine.
summary: Anne is exactly who you'd hope she'd be — funny, honest, and genuinely wise. She talks about shitty first drafts, why writing is really about paying attention, and how she deals with self-doubt.

timestamps.chapter: Why every first draft is terrible

transcript: Anne, your concept of the shitty first draft has helped millions of writers. Where did it come from? Honestly? My own terror. I was so paralyzed by perfectionism that I couldn't write a sentence...
